definition: Subtypes should be usable anywhere their base type is expected without breaking correctness or surprising callers.
ruleOfThumb: If a subclass needs special exceptions to the parent contract, the inheritance model is wrong.

citation.author: Robert C. Martin
citation.bookTitle: Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

questions.prompt: Why does this inheritance setup create surprising behavior for callers?

options.text: Code that works for Rectangle assumes width and height can change independently, but Square silently changes both, so callers get unexpected results
options.explanation: stretchWidth expects separate setters to stay separate. Square changes that contract, so replacing Rectangle with Square breaks caller expectations.

options.text: Square should be sealed so no class can extend it further
options.explanation: Sealing might limit future inheritance, but it does not solve the immediate substitutability problem between Rectangle and Square.

options.text: The issue is that area() should be abstract in Rectangle
options.explanation: Abstract vs concrete area() is unrelated. The key issue is behavioral contract mismatch in setters.

options.text: The problem is performance because Square calls two superclass methods for each setter
options.explanation: Extra calls are negligible here. The real danger is incorrect behavior when callers rely on Rectangle semantics.

questions.prompt: A service accepts any repository implementing BaseRepo. What contract break makes this subtype dangerous?

options.text: The subtype strengthens preconditions by rejecting a call that base-type clients assume is valid, so replacement breaks normal usage
options.explanation: register expects any BaseRepo to support save for valid users. ReadOnlyRepo violates that expectation by turning a normal operation into an exception.

options.text: The subtype should return a boolean status from save instead of throwing
options.explanation: Return style may be improved, but the issue is that the subtype cannot honor the parent contract at all.

options.text: The function register should catch all errors to make substitution safe
options.explanation: Defensive catch blocks hide symptoms. They do not fix a subtype that violates promised behavior.

options.text: Interfaces are the issue; using abstract classes would prevent this design problem
options.explanation: Contract violations can happen with interfaces or abstract classes. The mechanism is not the root cause.

questions.prompt: Both approaches model online notifications. Which one is more likely to violate caller expectations when swapped in?

options.text: Version A — one implementation turns a normal send call into an exception path, so code expecting any Notifier to be callable can fail unexpectedly
options.explanation: In A, callers of Notifier.send may not be prepared for disabled mode to throw. In B, the contract explicitly models failure in the return value for all implementations.

options.text: Version B — returning false hides failure and therefore makes substitution less safe than throwing
options.explanation: Returning false is safe if it is part of the explicit contract. What matters is consistency across implementations, not throw vs return style.
